name: Stockout Cost (basic)
description: A first-order estimate of what a stockout event costs: the gross margin lost on units you could not supply, plus any direct penalty costs the shortage triggered (expediting fees, service-level fines).

inputs.name: Units short
inputs.description: Demand that could not be filled from stock during the stockout.

inputs.name: Lost gross margin per unit
inputs.description: Gross margin foregone on each unit of unfilled demand.

inputs.name: Penalty costs
inputs.description: Direct extra costs triggered by the shortage: expediting, SLA fines, substitution costs. Defaults to 0.

expression: lost_margin = units_short * GM_unit; stockout_cost = lost_margin + penalty
assumptions: All unfilled demand is treated as lost sales at full gross margin.
Penalty costs are known, direct, and attributable to this stockout event.
One-off event view; it does not annualise or amortise costs.
limitations: Ignores long-term effects: customer loss, brand-switching, and eroded goodwill can far exceed the immediate lost margin.
Backordered demand differs from lost sales: a backorder recovers the margin but adds handling and expediting costs instead.
The exact cost of a stockout is difficult to determine; treat the result as a planning estimate, not an accounting figure.

examples.note: 120 units of unfilled demand at $18 gross margin each = $2,160 lost margin; a $500 expediting/penalty charge brings the estimated stockout cost to $2,660.

sources: Standard lost-sales stockout-cost decomposition (lost margin + direct penalties)

citations.quote: An important cost associated with inventory is the cost of a stock out. Although the exact cost of a stock out is difficult to determine, the cumulative effects of stock outs on the business are significant. A stock out occurs when demand for a product exceeds the available inventory. ... A stock out will generate a variety of different costs. Among the most important are: Lost sales.
the expected cost of lost sales per unit of out of stock

At this point the cost of the stockout depends on my behavior. ... the worst case for the cereal X manufacturer is for me to buy the private label after the brand X stockout and decide that I like the private label better.
